From December, two thousand eleven to June, two thousand nineteen, four hundred robotic-assisted colorectal operations were performed by a single surgeon at Taipei Medical University Hospital.
All procedures were using Da Vinci SI or XI operation system.
There were one hundred eighty seven women and two hundred thirteen men with an average age of fifty six point eight year old.
The mean body mass index was twenty three point eight kilogram per square meter.
Rectal surgery accounted for seventy four point eight percent of robotic assisted surgery.
Robotic assisted surgery complications include mechanical or equipment failures, user errors, maintenance issues, and others.

Soft tissue entrapment.
At some point during the operation, soft tissue may be entrapped at the joint of the instrument.
It is important not to panic because once the operator finds the right angle, the soft tissue can be released from the joint.
The surgeon slowly rotates the instrument to find the optimal angle for releasing soft tissue from the joint.
The table assistant attempts to pull the soft tissue forcefully, which visually risks causing trauma and bleeding in the surgical field.

The surgeon inserts an anal tube into the rectum for decompression, ensuring proper placement to alleviate pressure within the colorectal region.
The surgeon accidentally clamps the anal tube along with the rectum, without first removing the anal tube.

When this happens, the surgeon might be caught off guard, but it is crucial to respond quickly and precisely.

Sword fighting.
When surgeons operate, they may encounter uncontrolled arms.
The surgeon is seated at the computer console near the operating table, controlling the robotic arm during the procedure.
External collisions occurs when the instruments are inserted through ports that are too close together.

While the console gives the surgeon a high-definition magnified, three-D view of the surgical sites, the surgeon won't be able to notice that the arms with surgical instruments are overlapped, which causes sword fighting.
In this case, unnecessary tension might be built up.
It could potentially increase the risk of arm jumping, causing the instrument to puncture an artery.

Appropriate work processes should be planned to minimize the possibility of complications, including, first, standardized training program and conditioning guidelines, such as teacher-residents, fellows, and faculties.
Second, strict internal audits and reporting system, by developing a more uniform system of overall reporting.
In addition, proper disclosure of risks to patients is crucial, including possible robotic-related complications or potential conversions to open surgery.
